title: Separation & Authorization
subtitle: Four eyes, limits, and who's allowed

hook.metaphor: Nuclear missiles famously need two people to turn two keys at once — no single person can launch. That's the most dramatic version of the most important control idea there is: for anything that really matters, don't let one person do it alone. Most fraud and most catastrophic errors trace back to a single unchecked pair of hands.
hook.scenario: A manager can create a new supplier in the system, approve invoices to that supplier, and release the payment — all by herself. She invents 'Acme Supplies', approves its invoices, and pays herself. Which control would have made this impossible?

learn: The cornerstone is **segregation of duties (SoD)**: split any sensitive process so that no one person controls it end-to-end. The classic split is **initiate / approve / record / reconcile** done by *different* people. Our fraudster could do all of it alone — collapse those into one role and you've built a fraud machine.

Close cousin: the **four-eyes principle** (a.k.a. dual control / maker-checker) — important actions need a second person to review and approve. Two sets of eyes catch both fraud (needs collusion now) and honest mistakes (someone double-checks).

**Authorization limits** decide *who can approve what*: a junior can sign off ₹10,000, a manager ₹1,00,000, the board ₹1 crore. Limits ensure the size of a decision matches the seniority approving it, so no one can unilaterally commit beyond their level.

And underpinning all of it: **access controls** — the digital version of locks and keys. People should have the *least privilege* needed to do their job, access should be reviewed regularly, and leavers' access revoked promptly. A perfect SoD design means nothing if everyone secretly has admin rights to override it.
bridge: So at work, you can sniff out the single most common control weakness with one question: *can one person take a sensitive action from start to finish with nobody else involved?* If yes, you've found a fraud-and-error hotspot. The fixes are reliable and well-known: segregate the duties, require four eyes, set authorization limits, and lock down access to least privilege.

interactions.title: The fake supplier
interactions.prompt: One manager could create a supplier, approve its invoices, and pay it — all alone. The control that stops this?

options.label: Trust her more and audit once a decade.
options.feedback: Trust plus a once-a-decade look is exactly how this fraud ran for years. The problem is structural: one person owns the whole chain.

options.label: Segregate duties so different people set up suppliers, approve invoices, and pay.
options.feedback: Exactly. Once those steps require different people, the lone fraud is impossible without collusion, and honest errors get caught. Segregation of duties is the fix.

options.label: Let her keep all three but add a longer form to fill in.
options.feedback: Paperwork doesn't break the concentration of power. She still controls every step; a longer form just gives the fraud better documentation.

interactions.title: Separation toolkit
interactions.prompt: Match each control to what it does.

pairs.right: No one person controls a process end-to-end
A second person must review/approve key actions
Decision size must match the approver's seniority
People get only the access their job needs
Regularly checking and revoking unneeded access

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “For anything that matters, don't let one person do it alone — split the duties, require a second pair of eyes, set approval limits by seniority, and give people only the access they need.”

options: Splitting a process so no single person controls it end-to-end
checkpoint.explain: Separating initiate/approve/record/reconcile across people makes lone fraud and unchecked error far harder.

options: A second person reviews/approves important actions
checkpoint.explain: Dual control / maker-checker means a second set of eyes on key actions — catching fraud and mistakes.

options: People get only the access their role actually requires
checkpoint.explain: Minimise access to what's needed, review it regularly, and revoke promptly — or SoD can be silently bypassed.
